Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.

Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Very interesting! After clicking the blue square, more charcoal pixels appeared (now 4 total at cols 60-63). But I also notice that the charcoal square moved! 
Before: charcoal square at [44,46]-[49,49]
After: charcoal square at [44,50]-[49,53]
And the yellow strip next to it also moved:
Before: [44,50]-[49,51]
After: [44,54]-[49,55]
Also the white area on the right changed:
Before: [32,52]-[63,63] (368 pixels)
After: [32,56]-[63,63] (240 pixels)
And the dark gray area expanded:
Before: [1,0]-[63,51] (2580 pixels)
After: [1,0]-[63,55] (2600 pixels)
So it seems like clicking objects causes them to move! The charcoal square moved right by 4 columns, and the yellow strip next to it also moved right. The background areas adjusted accordingly.
But wait - the top row still only shows charcoal pixels being added. Let me reconsider the whole mechanism.
Actually, I think I've been misunderstanding the game. Let me reconsider:
1. The pink bar at the top is a timer that counts down
2. Clicking objects moves them to the right
3. The charcoal pixels on the top bar might be unrelated to the click color
Let me look at this more carefully. The charcoal square was at cols 46-49, now at cols 50-53. That's a shift of +4 columns. The yellow strip was at cols 50-51, now at cols 54-55. Also +4 columns.
But the blue square I clicked was at [24,60]-[27,63]. Did it move? Let me check... it's still at [24,60]-[27,63]. So the blue square didn't move.
Hmm, so clicking the blue square didn't move the blue square, but it moved the charcoal square and yellow strip. That's strange.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the mechanism is:
- Clicking an object causes ALL objects to move in some direction
- Or clicking causes objects to the right of the click to move
- Or clicking causes objects below to move
The charcoal square and yellow strip are below the blue square. Maybe clicking causes objects below to move right?
Or maybe the mechanism is about the objects being "pushed" toward the right edge, and the charcoal pixels on top represent how many objects have reached the edge?
Let me think about this differently. The game seems to be about moving objects to the right. The charcoal square moved from cols 46-49 to 50-53 (right by 4). The yellow strip moved from 50-51 to 54-55 (right by 4). 
The blue squares are already at the right edge (cols 60-63), so they can't move further right.
Maybe the goal is to move all objects to the right edge? And the charcoal pixels on the top bar represent progress?
But then why did the charcoal pixels increase by 1 (from 3 to 4) when I clicked the blue square? If the blue square is already at the edge, maybe clicking it doesn't move anything but still consumes a step.
Wait, let me re-examine. Before the blue click:
- Charcoal pixels: 3 (cols 61-63)
After the blue click:
- Charcoal pixels: 4 (cols 60-63)
So the pink bar shrank by 1 more pixel (from 61 to 60), and one more charcoal pixel appeared. But the charcoal square moved right by 4 columns. 
Hmm, maybe the relationship is:
- Each click moves objects right by some amount
- The pink bar shrinks by the same amount
- The "gained" pixels become the color of the object that moved
But the charcoal pixels are always charcoal, regardless of which object I clicked. Unless... the charcoal pixels represent the charcoal square's movement specifically.
Let me reconsider: maybe the game is about moving the charcoal square to a specific position, and the top bar tracks its progress. The charcoal square started at cols 46-49 and is now at cols 50-53. Each click moves it right by 4 columns.
But I clicked on the blue square, not the charcoal square. So maybe clicking ANY object moves the charcoal square?
Or maybe the game is about clicking objects in a specific order to move them all to the right, and the goal is to have all objects aligned at the right edge?
Let me try clicking on the charcoal square directly to see if it moves further.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Key observations after clicking blue square:
# 1. Charcoal square moved RIGHT by 4 cols: [44,46]-[49,49] -> [44,50]-[49,53]
# 2. Yellow strip next to it also moved RIGHT by 4: [44,50]-[49,51] -> [44,54]-[49,55]
# 3. Blue squares stayed at edge (cols 60-63)
# 4. Top bar: pink shrank 61->60, charcoal pixels 3->4
# 5. Dark gray expanded right, white area on right shrank

# So clicking moves objects to the right! The charcoal square and its adjacent yellow strip moved.
# Let me click the charcoal square directly to see if it moves again
# Charcoal square now at [44,50]-[49,53], center at row 46, col 51
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 46, 'col': 51}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking an object moves it (and adjacent objects?) to the right"}, "object_taxonomy": {"P": "pink top bar (timer)", "G": "dark gray background", "W": "white background", "b": "blue 4x4 squares", "B": "black bars", "Y": "yellow strips", "c": "charcoal square"}, "hud_map": {"row_0": "pink bar with charcoal progress pixels at right"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Clicking objects moves them right. Charcoal square moved from cols 46-49 to 50-53 after blue click. Yellow strip also moved. Blue squares already at right edge.", "goal_model": "Move all objects to the right edge?", "recent_findings": ["Objects move right when clicked", "Charcoal square and yellow strip moved together", "Top bar tracks progress"], "open_questions": ["Do objects move when clicked directly?", "What is the win condition?"], "current_plan": "Click charcoal square to see if it moves further right", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
